text: You know, if there's a lesson to be
learned from the recent manufactured
outrage at the BAFTAs last week, it's
that there's a lot of people in this
world who like to pretend they're doing
good, who see words like empathy and
compassion as tools to be used when it
suits them, rather than principles to
live by, even when it might prove
difficult. But on the other hand, it's
nice to be reminded that there are in
fact good people in this world. people
who face seemingly insurmountable
challenges and fight unwinable battles
every day of their lives only to face
scorn and ridicule for their efforts.
Such is the case with I Swear, a movie
based on the true life story of John
Davidson, a workingclass man from
Scotland who suffered from Tourette's
syndrome most of his life. The story
takes us from his early struggles with
the condition as a boy, the devastating
effects it had on his life and the
people around him, his slow and
difficult battle for acceptance, and
finally his work to raise awareness of
the condition and help other people just
like him. And holy [ __ ] is it good.
Combining a superb cast who all deliver
brilliantly natural performances led by
Robert Aramo who might be doing the best
work of his career. Great writing and
direction that never loses its sense of
pace or tone and a story that takes you
on a narrative roller coaster with an
emotionally satisfying ending that never
feels contrived or Hollywoody. I Swear
is a superb movie that deserves all the
recognition it's getting. So, let us
discuss it in a bit more detail, shall
we? The story kicks off in Scotland in
1983 with a 12-year-old John Davidson
who's about to start high school. And
when we first meet him, he seems to have
everything going for him. He's confident
and popular, academically bright and
gifted at football to the point where
he's being scouted to play
professionally. Then, something a bit
odd happens during his first term at
high school. He starts to experience
ticks, strange little twitches and head
movements, noises that come from
nowhere. And before long, he starts
blurting out the kind of curses and
insults that would make an Australian
blush. Nobody seems to believe that this
is all involuntary. Soon he's in trouble
at school. His friendships vanish as
people distance themselves from him, and
even his family starts to unravel. Holy
[ __ ] it's morning mortal.
>> I tried putting it in the water if I
were you.
>> Sorry, I just got a bit distracted
there. Anyway, everything seems to be
falling apart around him, and the worst
part is that everyone blames Jon himself
for it to the point where he even tries
to end his own life out of sheer
despair. Needless to say, he survives
the attempt. But one thing is clear,
he's going to have a very difficult life
ahead of him. Flash forward to the
mid90s, and Jon's now a grown man, still
struggling with his condition, and still
living with his mom, who's clearly
burned out by this point and can't stand
being around him anymore. This is when
he runs into an old school friend who's
come home to care for his terminally ill
mother and invites Jon round for dinner.
Now, naturally, this is the kind of
scenario that Jon absolutely dreads
because he doesn't want to embarrass
himself around strangers. But it's also
where, for once, life throws him a bone
in the shape of Doy, his friend's
mother, who used to be a mental health
nurse and recognizes the struggle he's
going through. Rather than being
embarrassed or frustrated by his
Tourette's syndrome, she just accepts it
for what it is and encourages Jon to
accept it as well and stop apologizing
for something he absolutely can't
control. Before long, he's moved in with
them and started to rediscover some of
his old confidence and ambition. He
lands a job working at a local community
center and even strikes up a friendship
with Tommy, the elderly caretaker who
accepts his condition and doesn't judge
him for it. A simple humble life it
might be, but for Jon it feels like he's
taken gigantic leaps forwards. It's not
all smooth sailing though, and as hard
as he tries, he can't escape the
condition that's blighted most of his
life. Fights with people that he
accidentally insulted, run-ins with the
police, bullying, and constant mockery
are reminders that he'll never have a
truly normal life. And at one point, he
even ends up in hospital after a brutal
beatdown. Still, somehow, he finds a way
to pick himself up and keep going. And
as time passes, he becomes an advocate
for Tourette's sufferers all across the
country, advising police and local
councils on how best to deal with them
and working with young people suffering
from the condition. Just as his life was
once turned around by a few key people
with empathy and understanding, so he's
able to do the same thing for others.
And by 2019, his work has even earned
him an official award from the Queen. A
man who very nearly ended his own life
before it even began because nobody
understood him. now presented with one
of the highest awards in the country and
more importantly finally recognized for
who and what he is instead of being
defined by his condition. You know, I
really don't know where to start with I
swear except to get the obvious point
out of the way first. This movie made me
feel really [ __ ] good. It reminded me
that there are good people in this world
despite what you see on social media.
Many of them fighting battles that you
can't see. battles that don't have big
dramatic conclusions where everyone
lives happily ever after so much as
incremental wins that slowly turn the
tides. And I think that's really what I
Swear is getting at. John's story
doesn't have a typical Hollywood ending
where he magically overcomes his
condition or achieves some epic quest
he's been on his whole life. It's a
quieter, more grounded kind of story of
a man's ongoing fight for acceptance and
understanding. A fight that, as we
learned last week, never truly ends.
It's a harsh and unflinching movie a lot
of the time, and it never tries to
sugarcoat a story that was neither short
nor easy. But just like in real life,
it's the lows that make the highs so
satisfying. And this film has plenty of
both. You're rooting for Jon to succeed,
and you can't help but cheer every
little win. From landing a minimum wage
job to getting his own flat for the
first time, which makes the setbacks all
the more painful when they come. his
agonizing attempts to explain himself to
his teachers at school. The dinner that
gets ruined by his ticks. The savage
beaten he takes for a minor incident
that wasn't even his fault. A complex
tapestry of wins and losses that all add
up to a challenging but rewarding life.
And it's all hammered home by excellent
performances all around that feel so
grounded and natural. It's like you're
not even watching a movie. Maxine Peak
was awesome in Say Nothing, a show that
I'd definitely recommend if you've got
any interest in the troubles in Northern
Ireland. But she's even better here as
Doy, the maternal figure who steps up to
support Jon when it's clear his real
mother can't cope with them anymore.
Veteran actor Peter Mullen is equally
good as Tommy, the aging caretaker who
starts out as Jon's boss, but eventually
morphs into the fatherly figure he's
been lacking for most of his life.
There's just such a warmth and humanity
to the guy, you absolutely can't help
but like him. And just like the rest of
the cast, he never feels like an actor
in a movie. He talks and acts like a
real person, not a Hollywood faximile of
one. I know it sounds like the most
obvious advice in the world. Don't act
like you're acting, but it's amazing how
rarely filmmakers and performers can
actually deliver on it. All I can say is
you know it when you see it, I guess.
But the real star of the show is Robert
Aramario as Jon given what I can only
assume is the best performance of his
career. Perfectly capturing the
frustration, the elation, the exhaustion
of a man fighting a constant battle
against himself. Sometimes winning,
sometimes losing big time, but always
trying. And holy [ __ ] do you feel for
the guy? It's a shame that Araio was
best known to Americans for being in dog
[ __ ] like Rings of Power because damn,
man, he really proves his acting range
here. His Scottish accent's so good, I
actually had to check to see if he grew
up here. But it's his portrayal of
Tourette's itself with all the ticks and
twitches that come with it that really
impressed me. It would have been so easy
for an actor to ham it up and make it
look almost comical, but Araio nails it
perfectly. One of the most heartwarming
moments of the movie is when he sits
down in a car with a troubled teenager
suffering from the same condition, and
they both just let it out, riffing off
each other and just sharing a moment of
mutual understanding. And I guess that's
the big thing to take away from I Swear.
It's not a story of triumph for success.
It's a story about understanding.
Understanding a condition that seems
bizarre and nonsensical on the surface.
Understanding that not all disabilities
are as obvious as others. Understanding
that there's more to a person than the
label societies attached to them. If
there is anything good to come out of
the absolute [ __ ] storm swirling around
the BAFTA awards, it's that a lot more
people might feel inclined to watch this
excellent gem of a movie. And they
should. It hits American cinemas in a
few weeks. And if you get the chance,
I'd absolutely encourage you to give it
a go. You definitely will not regret it.
Anyway, that's all I've got for today.
Go away now.
